title: Quiz: Quantum Relative Entropy
description: Test your understanding of quantum relative entropy and its properties

questions.question: What is D(ρ || ρ) for any quantum state ρ?
questions.explanation: D(ρ || ρ) = Tr(ρ log ρ) - Tr(ρ log ρ) = 0. The relative entropy of a state to itself is always zero.

questions.question: For a qubit, D(|0⟩⟨0| || I/2) equals:
questions.explanation: D(ρ || I/d) = log₂(d) - S(ρ). For a pure qubit state: D = log₂(2) - 0 = 1 bit.

questions.question: Quantum relative entropy is symmetric: D(ρ || σ) = D(σ || ρ).
questions.explanation: False. Relative entropy is NOT symmetric. D(ρ || σ) ≠ D(σ || ρ) in general. It's a 'directed' measure of divergence.

questions.question: The monotonicity property D(E(ρ) || E(σ)) ≤ D(ρ || σ) implies:
questions.explanation: Monotonicity means processing (channels) can only decrease or preserve the ability to distinguish states. This is the quantum data processing inequality.

questions.question: When is D(ρ || σ) = +∞?

options.text: When ρ and σ are orthogonal
When supp(ρ) is not contained in supp(σ)
When ρ is pure and σ is mixed
Never - relative entropy is always finite

questions.explanation: D(ρ || σ) = +∞ when ρ has support on states where σ has zero probability. Mathematically, we need supp(ρ) ⊆ supp(σ) for finite divergence.

questions.question: In quantum hypothesis testing with states ρ and σ, the type-II error decreases as:
questions.explanation: Quantum Stein's lemma: the optimal type-II error decreases exponentially as 2^(-n·D(ρ||σ)), where D(ρ||σ) is the relative entropy.

questions.question: Pinsker's inequality states that D(ρ||σ) is lower bounded by a function of the trace distance.
questions.explanation: True. Pinsker's inequality: D(ρ||σ) ≥ (1/2ln2)||ρ-σ||₁². This connects information-theoretic and geometric measures.

questions.question: Which properties does quantum relative entropy satisfy? (Select all)
questions.explanation: a) Klein's inequality. b) FALSE - not symmetric. c) D is jointly convex. d) Data processing inequality holds.
